name: Raster Descent
one_liner: A tall video frame painted line-by-line down a portrait wall: a white-hot scanline sweeps downward resolving a raster of fine pixels, then each row dissolves into a stream of discrete IP packets and reassembles -- SDI-to-IP made literal and vertical.
visual_description: A 9:16 portrait panel on near-black ground. A bright horizontal scanline sweeps top-to-bottom, drawing the image one line at a time like a CRT raster painting down a tall screen. Above the active line the picture is resolved -- a shimmering field of fine cyan/green signal pixels with a subtle moving pattern; below it the panel is dark, waiting to be painted, with a faint phosphor afterglow trailing the sweep. The active scanline is white-hot and blooms. On a steady PTP beat the upper portion of each resolved row PACKETIZES: the solid video bar breaks into a marching train of discrete glowing packet-blocks (quantized cells with gaps) that drift sideways -- the act of putting essence into RTP packets -- and reassemble back into a clean line. At the moving boundary where the picture is freshly drawn, the three color channels tear apart by a few pixels (RGB chromatic separation) then re-register into clean white. A faint scanline/CRT overlay and film grain finish the broadcast look. It reads instantly as 'the video in video-over-IP,' caught mid-transmission between a solid frame and a packet stream, painting down a tall wall.
technique: Single self-contained GLSL TOP generator (no input) finished by a SEPARATE downsampled bloom chain. The generator is inherently 4K-cheap: it has NO loops at all -- it is pure per-pixel math (a procedural cell image from a couple of hash/sin terms, a raster-reveal gate, a phosphor afterglow exp(), a horizontal packetization via fract()/smoothstep, and an RGB chroma offset). Per-pixel cost is a small CONSTANT independent of resolution. Crisp scanline + packet edges use fwidth() for resolution-independence. Time-dependence via absTime (sweep position + image motion + PTP beat). The only change vs the old video-raster is composition (a top-to-bottom sweep is already vertical -- it maps perfectly to 9:16) and the bloom: the old shader's bloom was a full-res 13x13 tap loop (fatal at 4K). Here the bloom is its own chain (downsample 1/4 -> bright-pass+blur at low res -> composite-add), so glow cost is ~1/16 of full-res and constant at 4K.

network_plan.name: glsl_raster
network_plan.role: The generator. NO input -- MUST use uTDOutputInfo.res; NEVER uTD2DInfos. Procedural cell image + top-to-bottom raster reveal + phosphor afterglow + horizontal packetization + RGB chroma separation at the active line + PTP beat. NO loops (pure per-pixel math). Crisp edges via fwidth. outputresolution custom 720x1280, rgba16float. Three vec4 uniforms via the vec SEQUENCE (declared manually). Place nodeX 0, nodeY 0.

network_plan.name: res_down
network_plan.role: Downsample to 1/4 of output res for the cheap bloom. Input0 = glsl_raster. width/height = int(Outw/4)/int(Outh/4) so it tracks dev AND 4K. rgba16float. Place nodeX 300, nodeY -400.

network_plan.name: glsl_bloomblur
network_plan.role: Bright-pass + blur at 1/4 res (cheap, constant at 4K). Input0 = res_down. Bloom-only output. outputresolution = 1/4 of Outw/Outh. rgba16float. Place nodeX 600, nodeY -400.

network_plan.name: glsl_finish
network_plan.role: Composite-add the upscaled 1/4-res bloom over the sharp generator AND apply the cheap CRT finish (scanline overlay + grain) in one O(1) pass. in0 = glsl_raster (sharp), in1 = glsl_bloomblur (upscaled glow). NO blur loop here. outputresolutionmenu = input. rgba16float. Place nodeX 900, nodeY 0.

network_plan.name: out1
network_plan.role: Manifest output_op / capture target, named exactly out1. Viewer flagged. Place nodeX 1200, nodeY 0.

network_plan.name: glsl_raster_pixel
network_plan.role: Docked pixel shader for glsl_raster. See glsl[0]. extension frag.

network_plan.name: glsl_raster_compute
network_plan.role: Docked empty compute companion for glsl_raster.

network_plan.name: glsl_bloomblur_pixel
network_plan.role: Docked pixel shader for glsl_bloomblur. See glsl[1]. extension frag.

network_plan.name: glsl_bloomblur_compute
network_plan.role: Docked empty compute companion for glsl_bloomblur.

network_plan.name: glsl_finish_pixel
network_plan.role: Docked pixel shader for glsl_finish. See glsl[2]. extension frag.

network_plan.name: glsl_finish_compute
network_plan.role: Docked empty compute companion for glsl_finish.

network_plan.name: annotate_raster
network_plan.role: Teaching annotation over the generator (glsl_raster). Mode annotate, Opacity 0.3.

params.Bodytext: One generator GLSL TOP, NO loops -- pure per-pixel math. A white-hot scanline sweeps top-to-bottom drawing the image line by line (CRT raster down a tall screen) with a phosphor afterglow below the active line. On the PTP beat each drawn row PACKETIZES -- the solid video bar quantizes into discrete glowing packet-blocks that drift and reassemble (essence into RTP packets). RGB chroma tears at the active line then re-registers. The 'video' in video-over-IP, made literal and vertical. Per-pixel cost is constant, so it scales to 4K untouched.

network_plan.name: annotate_finish
network_plan.role: Teaching annotation over the bloom + finish + out (res_down, glsl_bloomblur, glsl_finish, out1). Mode annotate.

params.Bodytext: Bloom is a SEPARATE downsampled chain (downsample 1/4 -> blur there -> the finish pass adds the upscaled glow back), NOT the old full-res tap loop -- ~1/16 the cost, constant at 4K. The finish pass also lays a CRT scanline overlay + film grain. Terminates in out1.

exposed_params.name: Scanrate
exposed_params.label: Scan Rate
exposed_params.drives: uRaster.x + the time term uRaster.w -- how fast the raster scanline sweeps top-to-bottom (frame draw rate). 0 freezes the sweep (drops that time-dependence); higher = a fast refresh.

exposed_params.name: Linecount
exposed_params.label: Line Count
exposed_params.drives: uRaster.y (row quantization) + uFin.y (CRT scanline frequency) -- number of horizontal raster lines down the tall frame. Low = chunky retro scanlines; high = a fine modern raster.

exposed_params.name: Afterglow
exposed_params.label: Phosphor Afterglow
exposed_params.drives: uRaster.z -- length of the phosphor trail below the active scanline. 0 = a hard edge; higher = a long glowing CRT persistence tail.

exposed_params.name: Packetize
exposed_params.label: Packetize
exposed_params.drives: uPacket.x -- how strongly the upper portion of each drawn row breaks into discrete packet-blocks on the PTP beat (SDI->IP dissolve). 0 = a solid frame; 1 = full packetization.

exposed_params.name: Packetsize
exposed_params.label: Packet Size
exposed_params.drives: uPacket.y -- width of each packet-block (in screen UV). Small = many fine packets; large = chunky blocks. The granularity of the IP packet stream.

exposed_params.name: Driftspeed
exposed_params.label: Packet Drift
exposed_params.drives: uPacket.z -- how fast the packet-blocks drift sideways (toward reassembly). The transport motion across the row.

exposed_params.name: Chromasplit
exposed_params.label: Chroma Split
exposed_params.drives: uPacket.w -- strength of the RGB chromatic separation at the active scanline boundary (a vertical tear matching the vertical sweep). 0 = clean; higher = a pronounced broadcast color tear that re-registers away from the line.

exposed_params.name: Detail
exposed_params.label: Image Detail
exposed_params.drives: uLook.x -- fineness of the procedural source-image cells. Low = broad shapes; high = a dense fine-pixel field.

exposed_params.name: Palette
exposed_params.label: Palette Hue
exposed_params.drives: uLook.z -- rotates the cyclic signal palette of the source image. 0 = cyan/green signal look; sweeping shifts the chroma.

exposed_params.name: Ptprate
exposed_params.label: PTP Beat Rate
exposed_params.drives: uLook.y -- frequency of the PTP sync pulse that triggers each packetization burst. The transmission heartbeat.

exposed_params.name: Scanlines
exposed_params.label: CRT Scanlines
exposed_params.drives: uFin.x -- strength of the CRT scanline darkening overlay in the finish pass. 0 = clean digital; higher = a heavier broadcast-CRT texture.

exposed_params.name: Bloom
exposed_params.label: Bloom
exposed_params.drives: uBloom.x in glsl_bloomblur (downsampled chain) -- intensity of the bloom over the scanline and packet-blocks. The booth-scale glow knob.

exposed_params.name: Bloomwidth
exposed_params.label: Bloom Width
exposed_params.drives: uBloom.z -- blur step in the 1/4-res bloom pass. Wider = a softer halo, still cheap (low-res).

exposed_params.name: Grain
exposed_params.label: Grain
exposed_params.drives: uFin.z -- film-grain amount in the finish pass. Kills banding on the dark undrawn rows at booth scale.

exposed_params.name: Outw
exposed_params.label: Output Width
exposed_params.drives: Render width (generator + finish). The /4 bloom buffer auto-tracks. 720 dev, 2160 for 4K portrait.

exposed_params.name: Outh
exposed_params.label: Output Height
exposed_params.drives: Render height. 1280 dev, 3840 for 4K portrait. The /4 bloom buffer auto-tracks.

palette.ratio: ~85% dark ground + undrawn rows, ~15% saturated image signal + white-hot scanline; cyan/green lead, RGB-split is an accent event at the active line, not constant.

performance_notes: WHY IT STAYS CHEAP AT 4K: this is the simplest cost profile -- the generator has ZERO loops. It is pure per-pixel math: a couple of sourceImage() evals (each a hash + two sin terms), a step()/exp() raster-reveal + afterglow, a fract()/smoothstep packetization, and an RGB chroma offset. That per-pixel budget is a small fixed constant identical at 720x1280 and 2160x3840, so cost is O(pixels) with the smallest constant in the set besides essence-streams. Crisp scanline + packet edges via fwidth(), resolution-independent. The ONLY change needed for 4K was the bloom: the original video-raster used a full-res 13x13 tap loop (O(pixels*169), fatal at 4K). Here the bloom is a separate downsampled chain (1/4-res blur -> upscale -> add in the finish pass), touching ~1/16 the pixels at a ratio fixed across resolutions. BUDGET: generator ~= 2 sourceImage evals + ~6 cheap ops per pixel, no loops; the only loop is the 1/4-res blur, pinned at 1/16-scale. Gate get_project_performance before/after; very comfortable at 4K.
conference_fit: The literal 'video' motif, natively vertical -- a frame painting itself line by line DOWN a tall portrait wall, caught mid-transmission dissolving into IP packets and reassembling. It directly stages SDI->IP packetization (essence into RTP packets), the core of why ST2110 exists, in the broadcast-authentic CRT-scanline + RGB-chroma-tear language every video engineer knows. The top-to-bottom raster sweep is the most natural possible composition for a 9:16 portrait wall (a CRT always painted top-down). Dark, premium, on-brand restraint with color appearing only as the signal. Self-contained and cheap; a recognizable 'this is the video being transmitted' beat that complements the abstract fiber/packet pieces. Bonus: sourceImage() can be swapped for a real Movie File In to demo SDI->IP on actual footage.
build_notes:
GENERATOR CORRECTNESS: glsl_raster has NO input -- MUST use uTDOutputInfo.res; uTD2DInfos on a no-input generator is a compile error. outputresolution MUST be 'custom' + resolutionw/h (720x1280) or it renders 1x1. NOTE: glsl_raster has NO loops at all; glsl_bloomblur has the only loop, bounded by constant R=6 at 1/4 res. res_down/glsl_bloomblur/glsl_finish HAVE inputs so sTD2DInputs[0] is valid.

UNIFORMS: each glslTOP declares uniforms MANUALLY via the vec parameter SEQUENCE (op.seq.vec.numBlocks=N, vecNname, vecNtype='vec4', vecNvaluex..w expressions), NOT op.par.vec.

RESOLUTION TRACKING (4K story): Outw/Outh drive the generator + finish output (resolutionmenu='input' on finish) and the bloom buffer at 1/4 (int(Outw/4)/int(Outh/4) on res_down + glsl_bloomblur). 4K portrait: set Outw=2160, Outh=3840; bloom auto-stays at 540x960. Verify res_down reports 1/4 size.

CHEAP-BLOOM WIRING: glsl_raster -> res_down -> glsl_bloomblur (bloom-only) ; glsl_finish in0=glsl_raster (sharp), in1=glsl_bloomblur (upscaled glow) -- the finish pass adds them + applies the CRT overlay/grain ; glsl_finish -> out1. ALTERNATIVE: a Blur TOP (Pre-Shrink=1/4) + Level bright-pass can replace res_down+glsl_bloomblur.

CRISP EDGES: scanline + packet edges use fwidth() so they stay sharp at 4K. Verify in a 4K capture.

COOK DEMAND: time-dependent via absTime in uRaster.w + uLook.w. Cooks every frame only when out1 demanded; temp Execute DAT OUTSIDE the COMP cooking out1; verify with two captures + a real sleep. Scan Rate=0 freezes the sweep but uLook.w (PTP beat + image motion) still references absTime so the TOP keeps cooking -- correct.

LOOK CHECK: several blending modes (raster reveal, afterglow, packetize, chroma) -- MUST be judged by an actual capture (load visual-aesthetics). Verify the scanline reads clearly sweeping DOWN, the packetization is legible as discrete blocks (not mush), and the chroma tear re-registers. Tune Packet Size / Packetize against a real frame.

DOCKED DATS: glsl_raster + glsl_bloomblur + glsl_finish each dock pixel+compute+info; HUG all nine under their hosts in the SAME execute_python that creates each op, then get_network_layout. LF/space-indented shader source.

TDN ROUND-TRIP: all 16 params single-component (Int/Float) -> default->value seeding works on re-import (verified v6.0.26). Re-import into a throwaway COMP, confirm a couple .vals + out1 renders, destroy it. operator_count = all ops incl docked DATs (annotations not counted): ~15.
